text:
I know this is not the
shroomery.com
or somewhere else you would usually find a “trip report”, but maybe my experience can help anyone else who wants to raise their dose, but is struggling with it.
I think many people here have zero interest in a psychedelic trip. I had never tried psychedelics until I got this headache, and I probably would have never tried it otherwise. That’s unfortunate, because I think it has had a positive impact on my mental health. Even if my headache condition were permanently cured tomorrow, I would still want to take a nice strong trip every now and then after having experienced it.
With a “trip report”, I see most recreational users try to exaggerate or hype up their experiences. The point of the story is make it sound crazy and exciting, or “ultra spiritual”. We aren’t looking for that while just trying to medicate ourselves. I don’t want or need a crazy experience or a spiritual revelation at this frequency.
I’ll try to do just the opposite, I’ll tell a story of how I keep heroic doses bland, pleasant, and safe.
First off, I never started small. I noted that shrooms are very physically safe, asked a friend what a “respectable” dose is, and went with it. I started with 5 grams of Albino Penis Envy. Yes, that friend is a little bit of an ass, but I’m grateful for the end results anyway.
It was a very strange and interesting day, full of bouts of euphoria punctuated with existential dread. I was closed in my bedroom, accompanied by my cat and two small dogs. I did not have a sitter. Nothing bad happened, everything went back to normal after it passed. I don’t recommend skipping the sitter to anyone else, you should at least have someone else in the house.
I didn’t really know much about the neuroscience behind psychedelics at that point. So I just took the ride at face value, with no preconceived ideas. Just an unshakable faith in my physical safety and that things would fall back together after each session.
My subsequent trips were similar, until I decided to break up a dose to stretch my supply. 2.5 grams put me in a terrible state of anxiety and nervous fear. I dreaded using the other 2.5 grams, but my supplier had ghosted me.
Now I know a little more about the neuroscience. I am sure that I feel more comfortable at the higher doses because it actually turns off the “Default Mode Network”, a series of structures in your brain that are primarily responsible for the “narrator” in your head. If your consciousness can be described as combination of spotlight, representing your attention/sensory experience, and an operator/narrator purposefully shining it around, then my consciousness became free of the operator. It was just shining around with no guidance, wondrously random and chaotic. The part of brain responsible for limiting and organizing sensory input also took a little break, and I experienced sight and sound like I had never before.

text: Hello everyone.  It has been a long time since i have been on here and i feel a little guilty about that since some people on here have helped me a great deal.   I guess once the beast goes away i try to forget all about it.   So before i ended up using the LSD i had decent success with MM that decreased duration and severity without a doubt.  The problem was whenever i knew i really needed them i could not find any which was the case here.   July 25 of 2018 i was in the same situation and about a week in to my headaches.  Had oxygen but was in a very bad place so willing to try anything.  Never intended on taking lsd but i had a line on it fron someone i trusted so i went for it.   I wish i would have journaled exactly what happened but here is a basic overview.   Took my first dose which i have no way of verifying the amount as they were on Smarties candies.... I know.  It is not usually recommended to trip while you are in an extreme depression but i was desperate. This was also the first time the beast came in the summer so i was concerned about this as well.  My trip ended up being unbeleivable and spent most of it just walking around at night.  I had a profound spiritual experience that i can not put in to words but it changed me without a doubt.  The next day i had a slap back and a minor one the following day.  Waited five days and dosed again however a much smaller dose and had a mild slap back.   This was my last headache to date.  I also took one more after another 5 days just to be sure.  Now you might be saying thats great and it is but i haven't even shared the most amazing part of this yet.  July 25th is also the last day i took a drink.  I had been to treatment numerous times and had no intention of quitting at that moment but i woke with no desire to drink.  My withdrawls were mild but still notable and all this time beer was just sitting in my fridge.  Now i am an alcoholic who drinks every day when i do so this is incredible.  It was also the begining of my eating healthy.  I can't explain this either but woke up the next day and no longer wanted to eat red meat, butter, cheese,  processed food, ect.  And this was literally all that was in my apartment!  I lost over 30 pound in the following 4-5 months.   Whatever happened during that trip has for the most part  warn off and my not drinking and eating healthy takes a lot of work as opposed to the 6 months following my trip where it was automatic.  It still gave me a foundation to start making some big changes.   I still have no idea what happened to me that night. My name is Brendan Burns and I have been a cluster sufferer for just about 20 years now.  Today I am here to talk about the research I have come across and the experiences that I have had over the past 6 years treating my headaches with the dissociative hallucinogen, Ketamine Hydrochloride.
First off I figured I would tell you a bit about myself and my situation.  I had my first cluster when I was 17.  I was lucky enough to be correctly diagnosed within the first 4
years and like most people I was prescribed triptan medications. For a while this worked.  Eventually however, the clusters were getting worse and worse and I was needing a constant supply of triptans.  Not long after I had turned 24 a bit of strangeness stepped in--I started having grand mal seizures due to an undiagnosed gluten allergy.  At this time my clusters started getting much worse but now I was also experiencing other headaches that did not always fit the typical cluster headache profile.  In particular many of them would last longer and act more like a migraine, some sort of other TAC or sometimes a severe tension headache.
Eventually I overcame the seizures by changing my diet but the headaches have remained.  I mention all this because although I have been diagnosed with cluster headaches, I also seem to have some other sort of headache that can mimic cluster at times.   Early on it was hard for me to be able to tell the difference but these days I feel I have a handle on what is what a bit better.
I've had a prescription for an intranasal spray for about 6 years now.  Currently I am on 50 mg/ml and receive 100 ml that has to last me 30 days.  I also have to make sure that I give myself at least 5 days of no ketamine every month to ward off against tolerance and addiction.  At different times I have been on less or more of the drug.  When I was first prescribed it I was taking 25 mg/ml but taking it more often.  Eventually I found that I was going through the prescription way too fast with not nearly enough relief and the prescription was doubled to 50 mg/ml.  For quite a while I kept at this dosage and refilled it every 15 days or so, but for me it just ended up being too much too often.  I felt li

Hello, Cluster Busters!  I am the one they call Onglamesh, and I've been a skull crushing, writhing crybaby for 10 years.  This is my first time posting publicly about my most effective form of relief... I fear possible repercussions for coming out and saying what I'm going to say, but my ever increasing contemplation of the term "suicide headache" has convinced me that I
MUST
share this information... what if somebody on the brink of ending it all happens upon this and finds new hope?  I've decided that possibility is FAR more significant to me than the negative... if I get busted for busting, due to talking about how I do it, then so be it... at least I can feel like I may have helped somebody abandon the thought of taking their own life.  Because I can honestly say that I've thought about it more than enough times, and I may have done it if I hadn't stumbled upon such exquisite relief.
I learned about cluster headache only 2 years into my struggle.  I also learned immediately that all the medications offered by doctors are very pitifully ineffective for most of us, and that the majority of sufferers seek medical help but continue to suffer because the medical world gives less than a shit about us.  I learned that most doctors don't even know what CH is, and will misdiagnose you or refer you to a dozen specialists who also don't know what it is and send you on the scavenger hunt for ineffective drugs with really bad side effects.  So I must confess, I've never been to see a doctor about my condition, I gave up on that idea before trying... and for that, I thank all you brave souls who have been running the medical gauntlet and sharing your experiences with newcomers like me.
I've taken acetaminophen, ibuprofen, and naproxen as pathetic excuses for relief over the years.  I would take whichever one was handy, or combine the acetaminophen with one of the NSAIDs, knowing that it will barely do anything if anything at all... but rationalizing that it's better than doing nothing to try and stop the pain.  I've been given darvocet, morphine, dilaudid, hydrocodone, and probably various other pain meds when somebody has one to spare.  None of them took my pain away; if I'm lucky, they'd get me a little bit high so that I care a little bit less.  So I gave up on prescription drugs 7 years ago and just took the OTCs with little to no benefit.  I went through a period of alcoholism (now sober for 5 years) not caused by the headache, but certainly utilized at times to help me dissociate from it.  Sure, alcohol triggers the headache, but if you drink yourself into oblivion, you won't even remember that you had one.  The problem with that method is you still wake up feeling like the hot pokers are rigged up to a jackhammer.

Drug Companies Join Medical Psychedelic Movement—but Without the High
LSD and psilocybin in ‘magic mushrooms’ are among drugs getting redesigned
Psilocybin, found in mind-bending mushrooms, is one of the compounds researchers hope can be developed into drugs that are safe enough to be taken at home.
PHOTO:
JON AMON/THE DENVER POST/GETTY IMAGES
By
Daniela Hernandez

TEXT
Drug developers are designing new psychedelic compounds to
treat depression and other mental-health conditions
but skip the trip.
Mind-bending psychedelics including MDMA (aka “ecstasy”), “magic mushrooms” and LSD are being studied as potential
treatments for depression, post-traumatic stress disorder and addiction
. Dozens of companies and academic laboratories are also making changes to the structure of those drugs, or designing similar compounds, to take advantage of their therapeutic properties without the high.
Human research on the newly developed compounds is just getting started. Some researchers and analysts said the trip itself could be critical to the therapeutic benefit for patients.
Drugs including psilocybin and mescaline aren’t patentable because they occur naturally in mushrooms or plants. Others including LSD and MDMA were created in labs decades ago.
Global revenue from psychedelics
could reach $8 billion by 2027, L.E.K. Consulting estimated.
Psychedelic drugs are tightly regulated.
Some including MDMA impart effects that last for hours and require patient supervision during medical use.
Companies and researchers said creating new compounds with shorter-acting or non-hallucinogenic effects could lead to fewer restrictions, and make them easier for doctors and patients to use.
New drugs could also open their use to people who are currently excluded from psychedelic treatment for safety reasons, including a history of schizophrenia or some heart conditions, they said.
“We have to create medicines that are safe enough that people can take them at home and put them in their medicine cabinet,” said
David Olson, chief innovation officer at Delix Therapeutics, which is developing drugs similar to substances such as LSD and psilocybin but without the trip. The company said it raised some $100 million and plans to move two drugs into human trials next year.
Psilera Inc. says it is working on derivatives of psilocybin and the psychedelic DMT, but without the trip and with fewer heart-related side effects. HMNC Brain Health said it is conducting clinical trials on a version of
ketamine to treat depression

Couldn't be more relieved to be writing this post, but I'm delighted to report that I'm 9 days pain free, so I am feeling quite confident that the beast is back in the box for the foreseeable. I wasn't able to find a huge number of detailed bust reports for LSD, so I wanted to write up a report that was quite thorough, in case it's useful to someone (even if it's just future me!)
Context: I'm 27, male, based in London. This is my third cycle so far, so I'm still very new to ECH. My first two cycles were two years apart. In my first cycle, I received a diagnosis after 6 weeks, found Custerbusters after 8 weeks, and spent the following 2 weeks on a slightly manic LSD binge, after being quite desperate for some relief. The cycle ended after that, unsurprisingly, though I was never sure if it was the L or the cycle ending naturally.
After two years pain free, it returned in 2017. In that cycle, I only had access to MM. It took 5 weeks to break it, but got there in the end. I then had 2 and a half years pain free before my current cycle, which followed a missed maintenance dose and an errant glass of wine in June.
Day 1: My cycle with a relatively manageable K5. I went for a relatively modest ~40ug LSD, mostly sub-hallucinogenic, in accordance with the regimen as it stood in 2015. I experienced mild slapbacks (a pressure building in my forehead) as the effects wore off. In the days that followed, my attacks remained consistent in terms of pain, with frequency staying at/below 1 per day.
Day 5: I got hit with a K8, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. I dosed again afterwards, around ~50ug. When I came down, I was hit with a K8 slapback, and then a K9 the following day. Frequency was now up to around 2-3/day, often with one attack significantly worse than others.
Day 9: Dosed again, this time ~100ug, after coming across more up to date advice which suggested 'recreational doses' for busting with L. I decided to bring the bust forward a day, basically because I had to be functional the next day, but also because in my recreational use I'd found that my tolerance level was usually unnoticeable after 3 days off. Perhaps this was a bad call though, as I got hit with 2 and a half hours of K10, hands down the worst attack of my life. Decided after this that I needed to get hold of some O2, as the caffeine and hot showers weren't particularly helpful anymore.
Day 12: Finally was able to start the D3 regimen. Also started cutting chocolate from my diet and stopped skipping meals, as I began to suspect these were triggers.
Day 16: After several days of battling my doctor for it, I finally got my hands on some O2. Can't believe I went without it for the past 16 days, and the two cycles before this one! I felt giddy with relief after killing my first attack with it, made a mental note to ensure I had it from day 1 of my next cycle.
Day 17: Dosed again with around~100ug LSD, very slapback heavy trip but super manageable with my tank by my side.

I know my case will be anecdotal but I still wanted to share my experience in the hope that it will help others suffering from cluster headaches. I first started experiencing cluster headaches at age 21 directly following the death of both my sisters and my father in a commercial plane crash. The trauma from that is impossible to describe, but I believe it was a trigger that lead me to having cluster headaches. There hasn't been a lot of research between trauma and chronic pain conditions, but I believe that the cluster headaches were a physical manifestation of intense psychological pain.
I also started drinking lots of alcohol to cope with the loss I had experienced and I believe this was a huge trigger for cluster headaches. College became almost impossible for me after that even though I still managed to graduate. Being forced to leave class or work because your head is about to explode makes cluster headaches more than painful, it makes them debilitating. I luckily had it good compared to other accounts I have read on here. I would get cluster headaches for about 3 months out of the year with attacks happening around 3-4 times daily. This went on for the next 15 years.
The last time I had a cluster headache attack was 2 years ago and this is the longest I have ever been in remission. So what cured my headaches? Psilocybin mushrooms. When I was around 23 years old I went on the website Erowid.com to try and see if there was anecdotal evidence of illicit drugs helping my condition. I read post after post of people using DMT, LSD and psilocybin to cure cluster headaches so I tried them all. DMT was the most effective for being able to immediately abort a headache in around 10 minutes. Because DMT is smoked and not eaten it has a much faster activation time and literally saved my life. For the last 13 years every time I have a cluster headache attack I smoke DMT out of a vaporizer pen and the relief is almost immediate.
I ran into a problem the last time I had a cluster headache episode. The DMT was not working. This was a serious problem and I went into full blown panic mode. I contacted some friends and was able to get my hands on a literal pound of psilocybin mushrooms. The next time I had a cluster headache I started eating mushrooms and didn't stop until the pain went away. I was eating around 2 grams of mushrooms at a time every 4 hours or so but it was just barely making the headache subside. So I just kept eating more and more and more. After I had consumed around an 28 grams of mushrooms I completely lost mind. The pain also wasn't completely gone, so something in me forced me to just keep eating more mushrooms. Over the course of the next 2 days I ate a half pound of psilocybin mushrooms. I know that dosage sounds really hard to believe, but if you know about cluster headaches you will do ANYTHING to make it stop. I was then basically schizophrenic for the next 2 weeks. I stumbled across
this article
and have copied and pasted it since I figure most members here probably don't have a NY Times subscription.
Of course an intrigue is whether the holotropic breathwork that purportedly induces full on psychedelic states could also possibly serve as some sort of busting technique?
Breathing Their Way to an Altered State
As psychedelics move from the underground to mainstream medicine, clinicians aspiring to work in the field are inducing altered states with deep breathing.
By
Ernesto Londoño
Reporting from San Francisco
Published
Jan. 9, 2024
Updated
Jan. 10, 2024
The instructions were simple: Lying on cots while wearing eyeshades, participants were directed to take deep belly breaths without pause to the beat of fast-paced music booming from loudspeakers.
The exercise, they were told, had the potential to induce an altered state of consciousness so profound that breathers sometimes describe it as reliving the terrifying moment of their birth. Past participants claim to have caught glimpses of past lives.
A few minutes into the session, which lasted nearly three hours, several participants began to weep. Some shook their limbs wildly, looking possessed. An outsider walking in would have been startled by the scene.
But the dozens of attendees at the recent breathwork workshop in San Francisco were far from hippies or cult initiates. They were health care professionals completing the final step of a certificate program in psychedelic therapy.
The vigorous modality, known as holotropic breathwork, is offered at the end of an eight-month training to provide a lawful taste of the therapeutic potential and pitfalls of altered states of consciousness.
Dr. JJ Pursell, a naturopathic doctor from Oregon, was among the trainees who walked into the early October session skeptical that a couple of hours of intense breathing could induce anything close to a psychedelic trip. But she was stunned.
“The depth of what I experienced was so similar to psilocybin,” Dr. Pursell marveled, referring to the psychoactive compound in magic mushrooms. “It was trippy.”
Image
Students in a San Francisco breathwork seminar wept and shook their limbs. “The depth of what I experienced was so similar to psilocybin,” Dr. JJ Pursell recalled.
Credit...
Damien Maloney for The New York Times
A Promising Field of Study, Long Fallow
The demand for psychedelics as tools for healing has exploded in recent years, fueled by promising clinical studies, loosening drug laws and a growing number of celebrities who have come out as psychonauts. The N.F.L. star Aaron Rodgers described ayahuasca ceremonies as a catalyst for “
deeper self love
.” In her new autobiography, the actor
Jada Pinkett Smith
credited ayahuasca sessions with putting an end to her suicidal ideation. And ketamine, an anesthetic that induces a dissociative, psychedelic-like state, has become an
increasingly popular treatment
for depression among people who get it at clinics or self-medicate.

I thought I'd take a moment and pen my whole
Cluster Headache
story (as briefly as I can). I find I post all, or part of it, in a number of new member posts quite frequently when people ask if
Busting
really works, and if there is any hope out there to be had, so thought I'd post it here, then I can just drop a link to it in posts. Lazy, I know.
Migraines
run in my family. I've had them all my life, all my siblings have them, my nieces and nephews and my 2 children. They have just always been a part of our family. My triggers are normally: stress, being over tired, hunger, thirst, changes in weather, sudden cold, strong smells, bright lights, and probably a few other things. I would normally go a year or so with none, or very few migraines, then be in a stressful situation and have a bunch.
In 2006/2007 I transitioned from male to female. In 2006 I started on a high dose of Oestrogen and an Anti-androgen designed to shut off my Testosterone. On 31 Dec 2006, at around 10pm, I got a headache. I wasn't ringing in the new year with friends that year. As chance would have it, I was home alone and not drinking. Just having a quiet night to myself. So the headache wasn't a result of alcohol or loud music or anything. Just a headache.
That headache lasted for
7 months,
and finally started breaking up in July when I'd got a few days here and there pain free. Then it got even better in August after I flew to Thailand for surgery. This was diagnosed as
Chronic Daily Headaches
(although at the time, it was in fact one non-stop headache).
That very first week of 2007 was shocking! On top of this headache, I got a bad
Migraine
and my first ever
Cluster Headache
. At that time in my life, my migraines were lasting 2-7 days, 3-4 days being the average. The
Cluster Headache
totally blew me away. I had no idea what it was.
I started out getting 2-5 clusters a week. I would have one Tuesday night at either 7pm or 7:30pm, another one or two on Thursdays, the occasional Wednesday and one or two over the weekend. That was pretty regular my first year. Plus, of course, nearly weekly migraines that were lasting days.
2007 was my Year of Hell. It was, quite literally, just variations in the level of pain for 7 months.
Thankfully, my GP had a good friend who had clusters, so she correctly diagnosed them on my first visit and got me straight to a neurologist. The neurologist said he had come across a number of transsexuals who got clusters. He believed that my body was predisposed to having them, and the change in my hormones just triggered it. Once triggered, there was no going back, unfortunately. It's pretty accepted that clusters are linked to the Hypothalamus, and also pretty accepted that changing one's hormones alters the Hypothalamus.

text:
Hi friends. This is going to be quite the wall of text, for a few reasons.
Partly to thoroughly explain my situation. Secondly there may be things in here that can help others.
But mainly, just to get things off my chest, or more aptly, out of my head.
I'll probably do this in 2 parts to break it up. First, I'll describe my background.
44 male from Australia. Suffered from episodic cluster headaches for about 15 years.
As with almost all of us sufferers, I have researched the hell out of this over the years, including here.
My cycle is a pretty standard one : I will get 8 weeks of daily clusters, and then a remission period of 1.5 years.
The first 2-3 cycles, i was pretty unaware. I was younger then, and simply battled through.
My go to was ( and always has ) a boiling hot shower, directly on the head, and rocking back and forward.
They hit like clockwork, once per day at 7-8pm every night, lasting around an hour.
I have a very high pain tolerance, but when these things struck, it was an eye opener.
I wasnt even aware a pain like this could exist. Things i used to think were a 7/10 on the pain scale, suddenly became a 3/10.
Its actually made it hard now, whenever somebody asks what a pain level is for a separate injury, im going off my cluster scale, because i know what a real 10/10 pain is now. My 4/10 pain, is their 8/10 pain. Anyway...
On about the 3rd cycle, it struck while I was out camping. Alone. In the middle of nowhere in the bush.
It was horrific. It was at this time, in desperation, I resorted to something i created out of desperation, called the rocking technique.
What i did, was kneel in the dirt (ground), lean forward and put my head on the ground in my hands.....and just rocked backwards and forwards.....and I counted each rock. As i said, I was desperate, with no other options...
It was during this, that I discovered, although it was very painful, if I kept counting, I never got to 500, before i felt it start to ebb away.
At 1 rock per second roughly, this was around 10 minutes or so.
I'd just completed my first abort! During the next attack, i was able to replicate it again.
Although still suffering up to a 7/10, it felt like a win.
But....
As I have discovered over the years, when you defeat the beast, it seems to piss it off. It tries to claw its way around your methods, or find a new way to punish you. I was about to find this out for the first time.
In the following days, my clusters stopped being like clock work. They started attacking at random times.
Worse still, my one attack a day, had now become 2-3 per day. Since that day, it has never returned to normal during a cycle.
Sometimes i wish i hadnt pissed ithe beast off, and just had the standard 1 per night, at a time I knew.
Regardless, for the remainder of that cycle, i was able to abort about 75% of the attacks using the rocking technique.
A year and a half passed, and the beast was back. Worse still, my rocking technique was far less effective.

Harmala Alkaloids are substances found in varying degrees in plants like Syrian Rue, Banisteriopsis caapi vine, Passionflower and Tobacco (I'm sure there are more)Â  The two with the highest known amounts are the Syrian Rue seeds and the Banisteriopsis Caapi vines.
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harmala_alkaloid
The major alkaloids involved are Harmine, Harmaline, and Tetrahydroharmine.Â  These are all Beta Carbolines, They all inhibit the MAO A enzyme, and they all are considered RIMA's--Reversible Inhibitors of MAO subtype A.Â (For more info on RIMA's you can look into the pharmaceutical anti-depressant moclobomide, which has MAO A enzyme as it's mechanism of action.)Â  Â All 3 substances are completely legal in the U.S., as are both the Caapi Vine and Rue seeds.Â  I know I have read that they are hitting some of the same receptors as Psilocybin and LSA, but right now I can't find my sources.
But first we'll touch on MAO inhibitors in general.Â  Everything you hear about them sounds scary, everything seems to say, "Do Not Mix With MAO inhibitors"Â  We need to know that the substances contained in Caapi vines and Syrian Rue seeds are very different drugs than the old style pharmaceutical MAO inhibitors that were usually used for depression.Â  These older MAO inhibitors would inhibit TWO enzymes, MAO A and MAO B, where as the RIMA's only inhibit ONE enzyme, MAO A.Â  This is important because that means you still have an active MAO B enzyme, which makes your body perfectly able to still metabolize Tyramine.Â  With the older MAO inhibitors that inhibited BOTH enzymes you are not able to metabolize tyramine, this can lead to hypertensive crisis, and this is the biggest warning you will hear about with MAO inhibiting drugs.Â  Just remember--THIS IS NOT AN ISSUE WITH THE NATURAL MAO A INHIBITING DRUGS LIKE RUE OR CAAPI VINES.Â  The biggest thing that made me more comfortable with these drugs was when I realized that I have been taking them all my life without knowing it.Â  Certain foods, like chocolate, have this effect naturally andÂ  you'll never notice it.Â  Â Â In fact, Terence Mckenna claims that ALL tryptamines are RIMA's--this means the mushrooms, LSD, and LSA containing seeds that we are taking are all having this effect on us.Â  I can confirm this effect from the experience of smoking DMT while on mushrooms.Â  (More on that later)
So no risk of "hypertensive crisis"Â  Just forget about it.Â  There is the theory that if you are on RIMA's and eat lots and lots of tyramine containing foods that eventually you could "overwhelm" the system and cause this crisis, but I have NEVER heard of this actually happening.Â  From what I've read it seems more like people have been scared of MAO inhibitors for a long time and are reluctant to let go of that fear.Â  One of the biggest sources of tyramine is chees

Hello everyone,
TL:DR
I may have discovered the cause of cluster headaches and have a working treatment plan, when previously very little worked for me. I went from fully chronic to them being almost completely gone. I've intentionally brought them back a few times, and then re-suppressed them to make sure it wasn't just luck.
These things have a cumulative effect, so while no one thing works well, in tandem there should be significant improvement. If you are desperate for relief, please give this a try:
Try clomiphene citrate, even if you've tried it before
Stay hydrated, drink lots of water
Listen to music in the mornings, especially music that gives you chills
Take 5-HTP in the afternoons
Try to destress / relax some - if an attack is triggered, try to remain calm and focus on breathing
Even if you've tried it before, start the Vitamin D regimen with all the cofactors asap. With everything, be very careful there's little to no soy in it. Some Vit. K gels are mostly soy. On sunny days, you may want to take more magnesium than usual, as your body is producing more Vit. D.
Lightly exercise as you can or go for a walk outdoors.
Avoid alcohol & sugar (including chocolate)
Avoid epinephrine (if you need dental work, share this with the dentist and ask for something without epi)
Try to minimize any opioid use (including kratom) if you have any for breakthrough pain. The only exception being Tramadol, as it can be helpful.
After being on the clomiphene citrate for a week or so, start tapering kudzu if you take it, as this can interfere with the clomiphene.
Be especially cautious of these things if you have a storm front approaching, as only one additional trigger will likely bring the beast back full strength.

I have been a chronic cluster headache sufferer since 2021.
Each year the condition has gotten worse and more frequent.
In 2023 I started my journey with the typical GP referral to neurology. Multiple diagnosis and failed medical trials later my condition really deteriorated in 2024. I was not having multiple attacks a day with little breaks between bouts.
Medical interventions.
Indomethacin - no effect
Lamotrigine ( I reacted extremely bad to this and seen a spike in attacks almost doubling and continuing like this for months)
Verapamil - suffered brachacardia so never made it to effective dosing
Atogepant - some partial relief from shadows
GON block - 1st one gave me 3 weeks the second one failed to have any effect
Multi cranial nerve block - both times gave me 5 weeks freedom
gamma core - no effect
Mitchell D. Post - Cluster Headache Patient Survey - 5-MeO-DALT.pdf
Alternative therapy
MM - months of trying to bust using 1 - 1.8g every 5 days to little effect.

Around September of 2025 I read the case study published by Jonathon Leighton from a recent study on psychedelics and CH.
I was 4 weeks post MCNB and knew it was only a matter of time before the beast would return.
Jonathon was kind enough to go into some more details and he sent me the document on 5-Meo-dalt which I had never even heard of. I figured it was worth a try and managed to get some sent to my friend’s mums house in Ireland (where it is legal) and then forward on to me in Scotland.
For me it was life changing. By the time I got the substance my attacks had indeed returned and I was back into a state of crisis having multiple 7-8-9/10 attacks a week and sometimes am and pm. I followed the protocol on the document and my first dose was on the 13th of September 2025. I kept dosing every 5 - 7 days and it took my attacks down to shadow like pain. My first real “attack” would be on the 30th of October and was around a 7/10 but only lasted 20 minutes. Fast forward now and the brief overview is 5MD took me out of the crisis state of daily excruciating attacks to the odd painful attack that was short lasting. I still suffered shadows on and off weekly but I had lots of days that I was pain free. I hadn’t lived like this for around 1 year.
I am still so thankful for Jonathon for sharing the document and introducing me to 5MD. It’s not perfect and I’m still on my search for a full remission but I’m living life.
This month I decided to take a break from 5MD and try LSD to see if I can get any closer to remission. If not I will go back to the 5MD. The beauty of this is it’s so mild at the doses mentioned on the case study (25 - 15mg) the experience is super mild. At 15mg I am barely aware of it so as a dad and business owner this really fits my hectic schedule.
Feel free to dm me or ask questions here. Im happy to share more of my experience and hopefully it can be of some use for others.

I felt compelled to share my story after seeing Bob Wold present at Horizons 2009 tonight on vimeo. This is my first post. I welcome your comments. Please forgive me if I have inadvertently violated any forum rules or guidelines, or if I am repeating common knowledge.
NB: I am not a doctor. I am an opinionated total stranger writing under a pseudonym. Nothing I am saying could or should be construed as medical advice.
I started getting severe headaches when I was very young. One day, when I was about five, my parents gave me the usual 2 acetaminophen and sent me to bed. About 20 minutes later, I came out of my room in tears. Exasperated, they wanted to know why. I told them because I couldn't handle the pain, and that if felt like someone was stabbing me in the eyes. Off to the doctor we went.
There was never really an answer to the headache question. At first, I was prescribed Fioricet (acetaminophen+butalbital+caffeine).  The headaches were called tension, then migraine, then cluster, then migraine. I was more interested in ending the pain then having an accurate label.
I do also get normal headaches, which are a totally different beast. No eye pain, no light sensitivity. I pop 2 aspirin+acetaminophen+caffeine and go about my day. If they get bad but not really bad, my favorite self-remedy is standing in a warm shower in a dark bathroom with my face directly under the spray until it stops. I also do this during really bad ones.
Some of the worse headaches were the ones that woke me up. I would usually be somewhat confused; and I would often spend a while in bed in terrible pain before I would get up and take pills. Sometimes, the pain would be entangled in a dream, and I would think if I could solve a math problem, or dial a phone, or repot a plant (I can remember those three dreams to this day, and they happened between 10 and 25 years ago), the pain would stop.
Later in life, they switched me to Zomig (zolmitriptan). The Zomig works ~75% of the time. When it doesn't work, it makes the pain worse. Currently, my insurance won't pay for Zomig, and so I have sumatriptan (Imitrex), which I haven't had to try yet, as I have one Zomig left.
When I was about 19, I entered another stage. The headaches became more common and lasted longer. The pain was worse.
I distinctly remember the worse one I ever had (and I bet you do, too.) It was 1999, and I was 21. The pain started when I was at work, about 6 hours from the end of my shift. Stupidly, I had no pills with me. I was already in trouble over attendance, so I wanted to tough it out. I sat at my computer, and the pain got worse and worse. Finally, with 15 minutes left in my shift, I had to leave immediately, or I was going to vomit all over my cubicle. They said they would have to write me up. I said I didn't care.

This is the first time I have posted here.   I have been visiting the clusterbusters site off an on for the past couple of years - but I have never felt compelled to post anything here until now.  Every time I read a post where someone describes what they are going through I can relate.  Some of the stories here are really sad - and I hope these people take the busters' advice because its all good and has helped me immensely.  I have been reduced to tears reading many of the stories because I know what it is like, and I have had help and I am finally making progress and I there might be some light at the end of this very dark tunnel I have been living in for years,
I have been dealing with CH now for 13 years.  For the first couple of years I was told that I had a a severe case of Sinusitis and that was what was causing my pain.  The doc would give me some Avamys or some other nasal cortosoid, some antibiotics and send me on my way.  This 'worked' for a while as my clusters only lasted for a couple of  weeks and I figured that the sinus infection had passed and I was fine again.  This happened two to three times a year.  No biggie.   The attacks became worse and less tolerable as time went on.   I started to suspect that I did not have sinusitis - even though I felt like I had been kicked in the face and my sinuses were plugged.  At the time I did not have a regular doctor and if I showed up at the hospital or urgent care facility suffering from a CH I was often dismissed as 'drug seeking' and sent home, because the time that any doctor decided to see me the CH had passed and I was feeling 'normal' again.  I did eventually find a doctor who diagnosed me with CH, but he did not know too much about it and treated it like a standard migraine.   Maxalt as an abortive and amytripaline as a preventative - that didn't work, but at least I had a name for what I was dealing with now.  10 years ago the internet was not what it is now.  Cluster Headaches were less known and there were virtually no options for people.  My CH was episodic for 8 years - then they starting getting worse.  The cycles got longer and more frequent, until I got to the point where I am now.   Chronic CH - 4-6 times a day for the past  five years.  I get my 'breaks' from time to time where I will experience a one or two days CH free and I have good days (1 or 2) and I have really bad days (6+ attacks per day).

only taking Excedrin Migraine every morning and every time a headache comes around. TOOK ME FROM 3+ DOWN TO ONCE DAILY! (most days)

Hello everyone, this is my first post as I am new to the community. I’ll first share my story trying to be brief (I won’t/can't).
I’ve separated my post in a first presentation part, and in a second part where i talk about my recent busting experience. English is not my native language but don't hesitate to correct me if I am gramatically wrong.
My name is Serge, 32 years old, from Geneva in Switzerland. I am born from a Romanian father and a Guatemalan mother. I’ve been in couple for more than 10 years and married for almost 5. I have two boys 5 and 6 years old. I’ve had headaches from as long as I could remember, but i couldn’t identify patterns. They were just so bad and no doctor was able to understand it.
My cluster journey really started in september 2018, we went to spain in vacation and everyday I got terrible headaches. When we got back home, they were becoming worse, showing up at night, at work, it started having a really bad impact on my life. After two years of suffering almost everyday, i wasn’t strong enough to keep my job.
I was a gate and check-in supervisor at the local airport, I had a good carreer there on the rise, but it came to a stop. In this job, you need to be fully available, you work early and late, you can't be absent even for a few minutes if you're in pain, so it wasnt compatible with my shit condition.
I’ve kept trying to go back to work after some resting periods, and it never worked, the intense stress, hard schedules, made my pain worsening when back at work.
When you are on sick leave for more than 3 months, the swiss law requires people to apply for disability. therefore, during a very long process, I’ve been sent to make some medical and psychologicyl exams to definite if I can still work or not.
It was around the same time that a proper cluster headache diagnosis had been made. Before this i was suggested (and convinced) that I had arnold neuralgia, I’ve already had tried all the numerous migraine medications you know.I had already tried nerve decompression in the back of the head too, with no results of course.
In late 2020 the examinations i underwent concluded that i had borderline personality disorders, and confirmed the cluster headache diagnosis.
At this moment when I had this annoucement I was trying to work again at the airport, but it didn’t went well, my wife and I lost our 5 weeks old daughter and that sent me into an even deeper depression, leading to the end of my contract.

text:
Hi fellow sufferers,
Two months ago I was diagnosed with cluster headaches. I’m 29 years old. It started in mid-November. At first the attacks were “moderate” and always came at 10 a.m. After about a week, the intensity increased. The attacks became more severe and now came at 10 a.m. and 3 p.m., usually lasting 1–2 hours.
I felt it in my temple, my jaw, above my eye, below my eye, in my nose, at the back of my head, and on my scalp. It felt like someone was hammering a huge nail from top to bottom straight through my face. I could even feel it in my palate. I get goosebumps just thinking about it. I thought I was going to die or had a brain tumor or something similar. At first there were only the attacks, and afterwards I was pain-free again.
After 5–6 severe attacks, a one-sided constant pain above my eye set in, which lasted 24/7, and the attacks seemed to arise out of that. The pain first brought me to my family doctor, who couldn’t help and sent me to the hospital after three days with no improvement. At that point I was taking ibuprofen like candy with no effect. I still kept taking it because I thought: at some point it has to work.
At the hospital I described my symptoms to the neurologist: a blocked nose, a watery eye, and head and facial pain that I didn’t think a human being could have and survive. She immediately recognized it: cluster headaches. I was then treated with triptan injections. The attacks were stopped, but the constant headache remained.
I had never heard of this disease before and started reading about it while still in the hospital. I used to be a smoker and quit the same day. Later I was treated at a pain clinic that had experience with cluster headaches. I kept a pain diary and read more and more about it. I started supplementing D3K2. And I found out that bright light triggers the attacks. The intensity of the attacks became somewhat lower—until they finally disappeared after about two weeks. However, the constant headache remained and I began to develop very intense light sensitivity. From then on, a baseball cap and sunglasses were my best friends.
I remembered that I had something similar about three years ago, though not with this intensity of attacks. Back then they thought I had sinusitis and I thought my wisdom teeth were simply causing problems. It was already intense enough at the time that they did a CT scan and found nothing. But it wasn’t comparable to the attacks now, and back then I didn’t even realize they were “attacks.” But I do remember the same constant headache above the eye that lasted for weeks and then simply disappeared from one day to the next. That’s when it became clear to me that it had already been cluster back then, knocking for the first time.
To get rid of the constant headache and end the episode for good, the doctor at the pain clinic prescribed prednisolone. He said normally they would also add verapamil, but since the attacks were largely over, it wasn’t necessary (thank God). In t

Most contributors to this forum will be far more knowledgeable than I regarding both cluster headaches and those substances which have not as yet become mainstream weapons for the medical community in the fight against this uniquely painful affliction.
I told my 25-year-old son Michael's story on Clusterbusters sister site, Clusterheadaches. I repeat it here in case it could be of benefit to any sufferers out there looking for others with similar symptoms and histories who might not luck onto, as I did, the sister site. Bonkers is the screen name used on that site as well.
Michael got hit out of the blue one day while taking a shower. It knocked him to the ground, hurt like hell and scared the shit out of him. He was 12 years old. It wasn't the last one. We thought they were migraines since both my mother and I had those from our teens until we outgrew them at around 30. Our family doctor tried a variety of headache medicines on Michael over the next couple of years without success. He referred us to a Neurologist who, after conducting the usual tests, diagnosed the headaches as Clusters. He tried most, if not all, of the standard cluster remedies. Imitrex seemed to work best, but it too lost effectiveness over time. It got to the point where 2 shots did nothing. Michael got the most relief, and continues to get the most relief, from narcotics. Most of the time they deaden the pain enough that he doesn't black out. He's currently taking Methadone. Unlike the other narcotics, it hasn't required continually increasing dosages to be effective. The neuro continued experimenting with the various cluster remedies, including narcotics, until Michael was about 20 years old.
Then, he got into a particularly nasty car accident. He was life-flighted to the hospital with head injuries. Nothing too serious, but his headaches increased exponentially in both frequency and severity. He was uncontrollable during the spikes. Luckily, the pain usually increased to the point where he would black out. By now he was in the emergency room every couple of days where they've got some bigger guns. Nothing helped. They had to put him in a straight-jacket and eventually, with consent from the neuro, a drug-induced coma. They kept him there for a week. When Michael emerged from the coma he seemed better for awhile. But then they returned. Frustrated, the neuro decided Michael should take all the medicines at once! I don't think we even said goodbye. Degenerative disc disease and 5 fused vertebrae in my neck had me seeing a neck and back pain specialist who I trusted and considered a very good doctor. I brought my son to see him. He's been caring for Michael for the last 4 or 5 years
Michael's case is rare, I believe, in its severity. Not to trivialize the extreme suffering of others, but Michael's spikes often intensify until they result in his blacking out, sometimes as often as 30 times/day. He can't work. He can't go to school. He has no social life. He lays in bed, in a

First off, I want to say that it's a life-saver that this community exists. This is my first post, and I figured I'd begin by sharing my busting story.
At the age of 29, I have been a cluster headache sufferer for 7 years. My attacks are yearly, at the end of the summer, generally lasting around two months. September and October are my cluster months. I am currently in a cluster phase.
Last year was my first year of being able to acquire the necessary medicine to attempt cluster busting. My approach may have been somewhat unorthodox, but the results were miraculous.
In 2012, my first (and only) cluster headache came late in the evening. I ate a modest dose of mushrooms at the onset of the attack. This was enough to receive a mild hallucinogenic effect, so I would estimate that it was around a gram and a half consumed. Eating these mushrooms did not abort that individual headache. I recall laying in bed in severe pain for several hours until the headache finally passed and I was able to sleep.
The next day, I began taking a small dose of LSD early in the day. Since LSD and Psilocin both have a tendency to prevent sleep, and since cluster headaches seem to be tied with the hypothalamus and circadian rhythms, I figured that my best bet would be to take the treatment early in the day so that I would be ready for a reasonably early bedtime. I figured that regulating my sleep patterns during this time may play a key role in ending the attacks.
I proceeded to take these low doses of LSD every day for five days. During that time, I had only a few shadows. They honestly didn't even feel much like cluster headaches, or migraines even for that matter, because they weren't unilateral. They felt a bit dull like a sinus headache. (I don't know about anyone else, but this is usually how I feel right before a cluster headache is coming. They build up to full pain much more quickly than a migraine. I go from having a dull pain across the front of my head to a full on excruciating eye pain within about five to ten minutes.)
In 2012, the headaches never came back. I did not have a single cluster headache for the rest of the year. I felt that I had more or less found an easy and painless method of aborting episodes.
I still have complete confidence that treating with tryptamines is the best and most effective method for treating this condition. I have had great success in 2013 when compared to my previous years, although there have been some rebound headaches this year. I'm hoping that I can refine my approach for future years.
In 2013, I did not use any mushrooms in my initial week of treatment. That is the biggest difference in the way I treated. The first headache also came late in the evening, when I was almost ready for bed. I did not feel prepared for a "trip" or anything that would keep me from a decent bedtime, so I went ahead and went to bed with that headache.

I was researching the whole LSA to LSH thing a while back and never really found any definitive answers.Â  There are intelligent people that swear this conversion should not happen, there are intelligent people saying that it should.Â  (One thing I have never heard people claim is that Psilocybin is getting changed into LSH, pretty sure that is impossible)
One thing that I found quoted a number of times is that Owsley claimed that LSH is indistinguishable from LSD but after researching a bit I found that the LSH that Oswley was talking about was a different LSH than we are talking about here.
Also, I have never heard (and am really skeptical)Â  that the LSA is binding to acetaldehyde and changing to LSH in your body.Â  If it did, there would be no qualitative difference in any sort of peppermint conversion (assuming that actually works)---instead you would always just be getting an LSH trip.
In the mycoptopia link that was posted one guy writes what I think is happening.Â  Â
"When the seed is picked, it starts to decompose to yield the sedating LSA, which further decomposes to yield BUNK.
However once the seeds are decomposed enough, but prior to becoming bunk, their magic can be "revived" by the combination of an aldehyde (acetaldehyde, commonly; found in rum, sherry or fresh peppermint leaf) forming an adduct with the decomposed chemicals, thus re-forming the potent, stimulating LSH... or (whispers in the wind tell of a stable aldehyde in cinnamon essential oil) an even more potent adduct when mixed into the good, old, sacred-seed CWE."
The Cinnamon thing that people are talking about goes like this-instead of using acetaldehyde you use a type of aldehyde (there are a number of them) that is in cinnamon (cinnamaldehyde) that makes a somewhat different conversion with somewhat different results.Â  (all theory)Â  Here's some more info on that:
https://mycotopia.net/topic/70425-lsa-cinnamaldehyde-cinnamon-essential-oil/
The thing I would stress is that no one really knows what is being changed into what (if anything is being changed) and we won't know until we get good lab tests done on seeds before and after attempting this conversion.
What I can say for sure is that my experiences seem to indicate that SOMETHING is happening.Â  I have had seeds that knocked me down and sedated me more than almost anything else I have taken in my life and I have had seeds that reminded me of a light dose of LSD.Â  These experiences are so different that I know something is going on.Â  Only once have I had seeds that acted like LSD where I attempted no conversion but I washed it down with an alcoholic beverage which I later found out is high in acetdelhyde.Â
Native peoples have always claimed these seeds are visionary substances and to me LSA's sedating stupor does not fit that idea very well.Â  I suspect

This is my first post, so wanted to say hi and to tell my story.
So the first time i can remember getting one of these headaches i was 4 years old.  I didn't know what was happening at the time, but looking back to that experience, it followed the pattern that has continued for nearly 40 years.  I can look back over that time and pinpoint many major headaches.  A lot of missed birthdays, a lot of missed family events, laying on the bed and listening to everyone having a good time.  Just laying in the dark, trying to avoid screaming.  No medication has ever touched them.  My typical headache usually goes like so,  a stuffy feeling in the left side of my head.  It is very rare that it happens on the right side.  At this point i've learned i get a bit of a brain fog and have a hard time concentrating.  If anyone jokes with me at this point i don't understand them and take what they say literally.  Then the pain escalates a lot.  Then i get spikes behind my left eye, where all i can do are pull my hair,  bang my head, scream and cry.  The spikes last anywhere from 5 minutes to a half hour.  About the only thing that calms me down is getting in the tub with steaming hot water in a cold room.  Sometimes i can get to sleep this way but not during a spike.  After the spike calms down, the pain is still very high.  The spikes will come back again in anywhere between a few minutes to a half hour.  This cycle repeats throughout the headache duration.   Usually if i get them in the morning, the headache will go all day.  I try to sleep in between spikes, though that is still hard.  Sometimes by the evening, i can get some sleep and if i make it through the night,  i will usually wake up pain free, though lately, more and more of them have lasted more than one day.  If i get my headache during the day they usually last well in to the next day.  These headaches often occur on weekends, especially when i vary my sleep schedule, and usually when i sleep in.  Sometimes i am so in pain, i get nauseous and throw up, though that isn't hi everyone,
so having mentioned this in a previous thread, here's my experience using exercise to abort attacks.
i discovered this actually because i haven't been able to get myself a good O2 setup here in southern argentina.Â
a couple of years ago i got hit by an attack while in town (i live outside town, 5 miles up a gravel road).Â  i dropped everything and literally took off running full speed for the hospital, not far away, planning to walk in and say "give me oxygen immediately".Â  when i was a couple of blocks away, i felt the attack subsiding, and by the time i got to the front door, it was totally gone.Â  completely vanished.Â
next attack 2 days later i was back at home and decided to try the same strategy.Â  ran off at a full sprint.Â  15 minutes later, no pain.Â
over the rest of that cycle, which i later busted with LSD, and then another whole cycle last year which i was unable to bust, i used this to great success.Â  observations:
the earlier i start running, the better.Â  it's more effective, but also the less time the headache has to ramp up (mine typically take 15-20 min to reach full strength) means less time i spend running while in serious pain.
after about 5 minutes, i notice that the pain is no longer increasing, by 10 minutes it usually starts decreasing, and by 15 or 20 minutes it is usually gone.Â  but that varies by exertion level...
since pain relief appears directly tied to how much i exert myself, it only works if i run myself ragged.Â  running up the steepest, longest hill you have around proves the most effective.Â  times i've had to set off from somewhere at the top of a hill with only downhill as an option, it always takes longer to produce an effect, and it's usually once i run down the hill just to turn around and run back up that i start getting relief.Â  in flat country it helps to have a nice smooth road to really sprint on, because i can just go full speed ahead right out of the gate.Â  with no hill to go up you just have to push yourself to go as fast as you possibly can.Â  Â a trail through the woods, while prettier than asphalt, doesn't let you run as fast, so it takes longer to get to the "panting furiously, can't make your muscles go any faster" stage.
i've tried other things besides running and none of them seem able to get me to that "pushing 110%" stage.Â  on a bicycle maybe if you have a long straight road you can really push yourself on, but then you'd also have to be paying attention to going really fast on a road that may have cars, with your brain occupied by pain.Â  once i was at the beach and tried swimming in the ocean, but that didn't work either.Â  after 10 minutes came back to shore and ran.
motivation is an important factor.Â  dropping whatever you're doing (especially if it's relaxing) to suddenly push yourself like an olympic track athlete, every day, with no advance notice, gets tiring.Â  sometimes i've got big boots on that are awful to run in (i've starting carrying sneakers everywhere

Yup.Â  Â Mandragora Officinalis, The witchy root, always portrayed as something to be feared and shunned is proving to be sort of miraculous for me.Â  Admittedly, I have not taken any heroic doses of it, and I would be very hesitant to do so.
At one point it seems like Mandrake was given to people for damn close to everything.Â  One book I have claims that it was used as a sleeping agent, analgesic, anesthetic, antidote, abortifacient, aphrodisiac, and inebrient and the number of ailments it was given for is ridiculous.Â  The more I've looked into the Mandrake, 3 things stick out for it's main uses--sleeplessness, pain, and melancholy.Â
My experience has been this-A couple months ago I found a woman selling a transdermal Mandrake ointment.Â  The tropane alkaloids in Mandrake easily absorb through the skin if put into good carrier.Â  This was just Mandrake in a beeswax and olive oil mix.Â  I found it to be somewhat weak, but after using a bit of it I was surprised to find that it worked really well for my lower kip clusters.Â  I think for higher kip headaches it would take a bit, but I have my suspicions that it would work.Â  I was hesitant to go for a much higher dosage because I had just begin to get acquainted with her...I am definitely one to experiment, but I am also careful as hell when trying new things, especially this one because some people are know to be especially sensitive to these alkaloids.Â
Â I had numerous nights where I had a low kip cluster towards the evening-this is usually trouble for me, because if I go to bed like that most likely I'm going to wake up with a tough cluster.Â  These nights though, I spread the ointment on my pulse points, a little in my arm pits, my temples and forehead and it completely erased my cluster and I slept wonderfully.Â  A few of the nights I really thought I was going to not be able to sleep and was going to have to take the Temazapam I have for insomnia.Â  Completely erased my insomnia and my low grade cluster, and at somewhat low doses.Â
I now feel much more comfortable with the Mandrake, and I look forward to trying again. Right now I just have to wait until my order gets here.
The Tropane alkaloids in Mandrake are Scopolamine, atropine, apoatropine, L-hyoscyamine, mandragorine, cuscohygrine, norhyoscamine, some strange alkaloid that I can't even figure out how to type, and 3,6-ditigloyloxytropane.Â  These alkaloids can be very unpleasant and down right dangerous if too much is taken.Â  One of the biggest dangers ofÂ  these tropane alkaloids is that they can cause real hallucinations.Â  Not breathing walls and geometric patterns like LSD or mushrooms, more like scenarios and situations that are not happening but you are completely convinced that it is real.Â  All while also experiencing serious over heating and extreme dryness of your mouth.Â  Too much looks like no fun.Â
The actual effect I got was this--deep relaxation and somewhat stoned feeling, a nice giddiness, and a definite "tripiness".

I first started getting cluster headaches when I was in 3rd grade, around the same time I started puberty, which I know is young. I asked my mother if I could see a neurologist but she didn't believe the pain was severe enough to warrant it - which was incredibly demoralizing to me as a little kid.

Hi friends,
I'm not super active on here, but this community has been such a huge resource for me in so many ways.
I've been an episodic sufferer for the past 20 years, going into an attack cycle every 1-2 years for about 6 weeks. After going through a very negative medical experience with various doctors, specialists, brain scans, etc when I first started experiencing CH (where the final diagnosis ended up being migraines
), I largely avoided doctors until 2 years ago, when I was in my last cycle. I thought, "it's been 18 years, there must be new research/medicine to combat migraines," so I went to a neurologist. The neurologist ended up diagnosing me with Cluster Headaches, which opened up a whole new world to me - it introduced me to this wonderful forum and its amazing, empathetic community members. It was also then through the forum that I first learned about the busting technique, and so I tried it out - and it worked! However, that was towards the end of that cycle, so I can't be 100% certain the busting technique stopped the cycle, or if it just stopped on its own.
Fast forward to now, I am in another cycle - and it's a particularly brutal one. The night I got my first attack, I busted with 1g in tea form. I got an attack immediately afterwards. I continued to get really brutal attacks, averaging pain level 8, 9, or 10 out of 10 with awful nausea and vomiting, every night. As this is my first time busting at the onset of a cycle, I told myself this was normal and soldiered on.
I unfortunately used up the last of my MM with that 1st bust, so the 2nd bust (5 days later) was with an acid tab. Big mistake. I had the worst nausea and attack I've ever felt. It's really something else to be tripping during an attack. I continued to get brutal 8/9/10 level attacks every single day following the 2nd busting attempt.
I was able to procure some additional MM, so I ingested 1g for my 3rd busting attempting (5 days after the acid fiasco), and that actually seemed to have worked. I went a full 24 hours before having another attack, but did end up getting one (and a particularly bad one at that) the next evening. I've had 9/10 level attacks every single day since then.
I'm now 2 weeks into my current cycle, with about 4 more weeks to go, and I am at my wit's end. I was meant to bust tomorrow, but after last night's attack where I felt like I was being repeatedly stabbed in my head for 3 hours, I set up an appointment with a neurologist for this morning to get a script for prednisone and topamax - I took prednisone & verapamil during my last cycle, which worked immediately, but gave me awful side effects (particularly the verapamil). I was determined not to go the prescribed meds route this cycle, especially after I read accounts of verapamil turning some episodic sufferers into chronic, but I might have to, as I cannot stand another night of brain terror.
I don't want to give up on the busting technique just yet, but I am desperate for some relief. I kn

I would have posted this on the 11th, but I was in the hospital. So, I'm posting now.
My CH started on May 28th 2008. I was diagnosed pretty fast. An ENT who had saw me a few days before actually said my sinuses were clear, the best he'd ever seen, and he thought I had CH. I kinda blew it off, I'd read about them, and this sinus pain wasn't so bad. I think it was a shadow.
Chronic from the start, I'd have them every other day, around 12pm. Then eventually it moved to every day, and then to 3-8 times per day.
I tried most of the conventional drugs, and non-conventional, because I had so many allergies to things. I'm one of the lucky few that Narcotics work on, so my GP put me on Stadol. I had Radiofrequency Ablation to both occipital nerves and it made me wish I was dead.
I don't really feel like going through the details of this. So I'll skip ahead.
When I came here to clusterbusters, I was in hell. My doctors had pretty much crappity smacked me over and told me I had no options. When they told me this, I sobbed for a few days... and then I realized something.
If God/The creator/fate wanted me dead, there were a lot better ways to do it than to drive me to kill myself. No. I knew there was another option, the RC seeds, mushrooms, and LSD. I asked my Mom and Aunt if they knew how to get some, and they were shocked. "We're OLD! We don't hang around with people who do LSD anymore!"
I came to join Clusterbusters, and I was still in a bad place. If the first dose of seeds didn't change anything, I was going to put myself in the mental hospital. I know what suicide does to the people you leave behind. (A friend of my family's sister killed herself... and the things it did to them was horrible.)
The first use of the RC seeds, I felt tingling. Something was changing with my trigeminal nerve. The migraines I was having a few times a week stopped. Completely after the first dose.
I went from being in a place of "I'm not going to make it through this." to "I CAN survive it."
The CH got better and better with each dose, but I was still having to go to the ER pretty frequently when the hits got out of hand. Then it got to where I just could not get the seeds to go down.
An angel helped me get some Vitamin M, and for me, there are no words to say how thankful I am for that. It worked SO much better on me than the seeds.
In either June, or July of 2011, I finally made it into low cycle! I thought it was a myth, or at the very least, it didn't exist for me. Once I realized it, I was so happy.
Eventually I had to start growing my own.
I also eventually got to try LSD (which I'm also very thankful for. Just like the person who helped me with the Vitamin M, there are not enough words to convey my grattitude) and, from my experience, it works just as well for me as the mushrooms. 1/2 a tab has kept me in low cycle for almost 3 months. Mushrooms keep me in low about that long too.
Right now I'm having about 1-3 hits per day, pretty low level. I'm doing SO much better
topic: I've been busting for a year now!

text:
Hello. Long time lurker and CH sufferer here. I felt I should add my Aya story here as there seems to be somewhat of a dearth of DMT busting reports. Background: diagnosed with CH 3 years ago and entered a CH period 3 months ago, the worst to date with a constant phantom headache + nightly attacks that were terrifying and excruciating. Imitrex injections were my only relief, preventatives seemed to not help and upset my stomache greatly. O2 was effective at preventing a bad headache from becoming an awful one, but had very little effect on a full blown attack. I had begun to think I had gone chronic, and my wallet couldn't handle much more Imitrex so I turned to this site...
I need to state that I have extensive experience with various hallucinogens including LSD and psilocybes in my younger years, and maintain a tremendous amount of respect for this medicine. It is not "recreational" in my mind, and I do not apologize for embracing the teaching aspects, whether awesome or terrifying, of a psychadelic experience. I will also state that Aya is NOT to be taken lightly...
A friend of a friend located the magic root bark + necessary MAOI seeds, and the brew was prepared in a contemporary fashion. At the time of ingesting the MAOI and up to the point of ingesting the DMT containing brew, I had a significant phantom aching. This stopped within minutes of taking the brew. After about 25 minutes I "purged", which was a VERY unpleasant experience. The "ride" lasted about 2 hours, and I am very glad that I have experience with such things, as it was extremely intense, with powerful visuals. I am also glad my GF was with me, and glad that she experienced this ride with me. Despite my mind venturing to some pretty dark places, we both navigated the journey quite well, and I am grateful for the experience. And also grateful that I took an "average" size dose.
If someone is interested in the actual trip report I would post it, but the primary purpose of this report is to say that I have been symptom free since Saturday. This is significant to me because I have had not one pain free day since this cycle began. Could my cycle have coincidentally ended at the exact same time I drank the brew? maybe, but I highly doubt it. Also, as it has only been 3 full days, the jury is technically still out on this one. But I am very, very confident.
I am unsure of what the appropriate etiquette is, in regards to posting details of the ingredients and brewing method here, but if it is ok I would be happy to post that information, along with my feedback on dosage.
Again I must stress that Aya is a POWERFUL experience, and I would recommend quite a bit of self examination, and a good amount of time reading trip reports before undertaking. This is not to scare anyone off by any means. I feel that this was a life changing experience for the better, I have a clarity and appreciation for life that is difficult to describe right now, and it is not simply a matter of being pain free.

text:
I'm about 13 days from being able to Bust, so I thought I would start a journal of my experience. My goal is for it to help me, as I find writing theraputic, but also it may help others in the same way that I'm finding reading the posts and journals here so very helpful.
My brief backstory
31 Dec, 2006 at 21:50, I got a headache. It lasted until July 2007. Yes, 7 months, non-stop, headache.
I have had migraines all my life as they run in the family, so wasn't too shocked when that Jan & Feb was chocker-block with them. The clusters also started the first or second week of January 2007.
The first 2 or 3 years, the clusters lasted 30-45 minutes, sometimes an hour. They were also like clockwork. Frequently on a Tuesday, often at 19:00, or 19:40 (or both). But they could also be quite random when they struck. Year 3 and 4 they started getting shorter, 10, 15, 20 minutes on average. Sometimes mild, sometimes extreme. Always scary, always with side affects. That's how I know it's a cluster, and not an ice pick, or a migraine, or just a bad, short headache. Well, one of the ways.
This year, year 5, they've turned from episodic (where I had 2 weeks to a 2 month gap) to chronic.
Tracking the Demon
For the last 2 years, I have tracked every headache I've had, all the meds I've taken, etc. etc. I have daily chronic headaches 71% of the year, which works out to roughly 5 days out of 7. This year I've had 40 migraines, down from last year's 63. But my clusters have blasted through the roof this year. Last year I had a leasurly 63, this year (so far) 226.
I know this is no worse than many here, and my heart breaks when I read of what other's suffer with. But it is a measure of where I'm at today, and hopefully I'll have better stats to share in a year's time.
So, why am I here?
I've known about ClusterBusters for a long time, and the use of LSD and shrooms. But I always considered it extreme, a last resort. Then, the last few weeks, as my cluster count topped 200, I started fixing a date in my mind that if I couldn't find a way to improve things, I would end things. And suddenly, busting didn't seem so extreme.
My plans and intent
Today, I received over 200 RC seeds in the post. But I can't do anything with them until I am off of Topiramate, +5 days detox, which works out to about 16 Dec.
Also, I am going to start on the D3 Regimen just as soon as I'm able, but Topiramate is holding me back from that as well, as it seems if I take too much Vitamin D while on T, it turns my urine on fire. Which isn't fun, trust me.
Plus, if that isn't enough, I've arranged through a friend to get some shroom spores. So long-term, I have that to plan for as well. (and learn about)
So, hopefully, I now have all my bases covered, and 13 days to read everything I can read on RC seed and shrooms. I've learned so much already!
I want to thank everyone who has put such hard work into this site, and has already given me advice and hope.
